text: The prostate is retracted anteriorly towards the top of the screen, with the bladder neck visible behind it and the rectum located posteriorly.

The surgeon performs an incision to develop a plane anterior to Denonvilliers' fascia, using sharp and blunt dissection techniques. The midline view shows the creation of a plane posterior to the prostate, with the anterior leaflet of Denonvilliers' fascia visible behind the suction device, and the anterior rectal wall situated further behind.

Using the da Vinci XI robotic system, the surgeon rotates the thirty-degree scope to look upwards, providing a clear view of the posterior surface of the prostate. Sharp dissection is initially used to tease away the posterior prostatic branches, followed by blunt dissection to gradually peel away tissue, ensuring precise dissection along the prostate capsule without causing a capsulotomy.

The surgeon carefully peels away Denonvier's fascia to expose the posterior surface of the prostate, working laterally to release the neurovascular bundles from the posterior side. The dissection progresses from the patient's left to right side, moving towards the midline and approaching the posterior aspect of the urethra.

The surgeon carefully pushes back tissue strands, dividing any resistant ones sharply, while continuing dissection in a caudal and lateral direction. The neurovascular bundle on the right side is visibly released. The dissection progresses anterior to Denonvier's fascia, approaching the urethra just beyond the apex of the prostate, which is now visible. The procedure is performed slowly to preserve the nerve supply bilaterally.

The surgeon uses a Johan's grasper and the progress instrument to retract the prostate anteriorly, controlling the right seminal vesicle and vas. The prostate is lifted out of the pelvis, completing the posterior dissection with a thirty-degree downward view.

The surgeon uses a haemlock clip to secure the pedicle, creating space and thinning it out. The clip is placed in an antegrade fashion towards the base of the prostate, enhancing visibility of the plane between the neurovascular bundle and the prostate base. The left hand retracts the prostate towards the midline while the right hand gently teases away the neurovascular bundle, utilizing traction and counter-traction for better exposure.

The surgeon continues the dissection in an a-thermal fashion, carefully teasing away a small capsular arterial branch from the surface of the prostate. The dissection progresses in an antegrade fashion towards the apex, revealing a bulky, well-preserved neurovascular bundle on the right side.

The surgeon mobilizes the neurovascular bundle down towards the apex of the prostate on the right side. The procedure then shifts to the contralateral side, where the left seminal vesicle and vas are grasped to retract and elevate the prostate. The posterior aspect of the prostate is visualized and elevated using monopolar sutures, and the area for the next hemlock clip is identified to control the vascular pedicle on the left side. Each clip application allows further elevation and direct visualization of the posterior contour of the prostate.

The surgeon applies a Hemolock clip to the identified pedicle, facilitating the release of the pedicle and highlighting the neurovascular bundle. The previously dissected plane from the posterior dissection is visible, aiding in the anti-grade nerve release.

The surgeon uses spot bipolar diathermy to control a small capsular branch as the dissection progresses towards the mid prostatic area. Counter traction is applied using a Johan retractor, revealing the basal contour of the prostate on the left side. The neurovascular bundle is protected and retracted with a bipolar grasper, while cold scissors are used to divide the anterior reflection of the lateral prostatic fascia. The incision continues down towards the left puboprostatic ligament, preserving the neurovascular bundle and allowing clear visualization of the apex for further dissection.

The surgeon approaches the dorsal venous complex (DVC) on the left side, where the nerve has been released. The anterior aspect of the prostate is secured, and the anterior lateral prostatic fascia is released. The neurovascular bundle is retracted using the assistant sucker. A large vein is visible at the top of the high lateral prostatic fascia release, preserved in continuity with the neurovascular bundle. The DVC is being divided at the twelve o'clock position in the image.

The surgeon uses electrocautery (Cortri) to facilitate the release of tissue, progressing towards the apex of the prostate and down to the urethra in the midline. The procedure is performed in a controlled, stepwise manner to ensure oncological control and preserve critical structures for continence and sexual function recovery. The high lateral release of the neurovascular bundle is visible bilaterally.

The surgeon is nearly through the dorsal venous complex (DVC), preparing to expose the urethra. Hemostatic control is maintained with a previously placed suture, ensuring clear visualization and preventing accidental entry into the prostate apex.

The surgeon accurately releases the neurovascular bundle bilaterally, exposing the urethra. A catheter is visible as it is inserted through the urethra, and the anterior surface is incised to reveal the catheter. The posterior aspect of the urethra is divided, and the apex is dissected free, providing a clear view of the preserved neurovascular bundles. The catheter is reinserted through the urethra for a hemostatic check.
